spec_match.rationale: The criteria table is now paired with representative ChromeStatus categories: common analytics scripts, social embeds, video player embeds, captcha providers, ads libraries, and explicit non-candidates such as pinned framework URLs and self-hosted wrappers.

interactive.rationale: The generic capability probe was replaced with a URL/fetch-entry allowlist matcher that parses the resource, checks provider category, HTTPS, self-hosting, manually pinned framework/library paths, and user/session query parameters.

blurb_alignment.rationale: The page now explains what makes a resource pervasive, gives worked qualifying and non-qualifying examples, and keeps the privacy/performance tradeoff section.

edge_cases.rationale: The non-qualifying table is good but misses edge cases the spec actually wrestles with: versioned URLs where versioning is manual (excluded per chromestatus), content-dictionary resources (explicitly included), and the cookie-Vary interaction.

references.rationale: Links to https://chromestatus.com/feature/5202380930678784 in the see-also section.

design_system.rationale: All colours via CSS variables, criteria tables use accent-emerald/accent-rose for yes/no with sufficient contrast, no button-colour regression.

openQuestions.title: Explain the HTTP Archive curation step
openQuestions.detail: Chromestatus says the allowlist is 'curated from the HTTP Archive dataset'. That is the answer to 'how do I get my CDN on the list?' and deserves a paragraph — describe the HTTP Archive query, the cadence of curation, and where developers can see/influence the list.

openQuestions.title: Tie the privacy tradeoff to a concrete cache-timing attack
openQuestions.detail: The tradeoff card claims 'cache timing reveals presence/absence — but these resources are always present' as the safety argument. Concretise it: link to or summarise the 2014 Smith/Snyder paper on cache-timing attacks so the reader understands what was prevented in Chrome 86 and why this carve-out is safe.

summary: The page now has a real allowlist matcher and worked examples for the ChromeStatus categories, while retaining the qualification, exclusion, and privacy/performance reference material.
